heroOverview: Diabetic retinopathy is damage to the light-sensing retina caused by chronically elevated blood glucose, which weakens and distorts the small blood vessels that nourish retinal tissue. It is the leading cause of new blindness among working-age adults worldwide, affecting an estimated 103 million people as of 2020. The condition is silent in its early stages — vision typically remains normal until significant damage has accumulated. After 20 years of diabetes, more than 60% of people with type 2 diabetes and nearly all with type 1 show some degree of retinal involvement. Tight glycaemic and blood-pressure control can halt or slow progression at every stage, and laser photocoagulation, intravitreal anti-VEGF injections, and vitreoretinal surgery can preserve sight even in advanced disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 103 million people affected globally (2020); present in ~34% of people with diabetes worldwide (Teo et al., Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Leading cause of new blindness in working-age adults (20–74 years); more common with longer duration of diabetes
keyStats.avgAge: Risk rises steeply after 5–10 years of diabetes regardless of age at diagnosis
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 28 million people have vision-threatening diabetic retinopathy globally

definition: Diabetic retinopathy (ICD-10: E11.31–E11.359 for type 2; E10.31–E10.359 for type 1) is a microvascular complication of diabetes mellitus in which sustained hyperglycaemia damages the pericytes, endothelial cells, and basement membranes of the retinal capillaries. The result is a spectrum of pathology ranging from subclinical microaneurysms to frank retinal ischaemia, neovascularisation, and traction detachment. Two principal forms are recognised: non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy (NPDR), in which abnormalities remain confined to the retinal vasculature, and proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR), where ischaemia drives the growth of fragile new vessels on the retinal surface or into the vitreous. Diabetic macular oedema (DMO) — swelling of the central retina — can occur at any stage and is the commonest cause of vision loss from the condition. The American Academy of Ophthalmology (AAO) Preferred Practice Pattern classifies NPDR as mild, moderate, or severe based on microaneurysm count, haemorrhage extent, venous beading, and intraretinal microvascular abnormalities. Globally, the International Diabetes Federation estimates that approximately 537 million adults lived with diabetes in 2021, placing enormous numbers at long-term risk of retinopathy.

typesClassification.description: Characterised by scattered microaneurysms — tiny balloon-like swellings in capillary walls — and occasional small dot haemorrhages. Vision is rarely affected, and the natural history is slow progression over years.
More extensive microaneurysms, haemorrhages, hard exudates (leaking lipid), and early venous changes. Progression to severe NPDR or PDR over two years occurs in roughly 15–25% of untreated eyes.

Defined by the 4-2-1 rule: extensive haemorrhages in all four quadrants, venous beading in two quadrants, or intraretinal microvascular abnormalities in one quadrant. About half of untreated eyes reach PDR within a year.

Retinal ischaemia triggers neovascularisation — new fragile vessels on the disc or elsewhere in the retina. These can bleed into the vitreous (causing sudden vision loss) or contract to produce traction retinal detachment.

Fluid accumulation at the macula — the region responsible for central fine-detail vision — from leaking retinal vessels. Clinically significant DMO reduces reading and driving vision and can complicate any stage of retinopathy.

primarySymptoms: Gradual blurring of central vision, particularly for reading or recognising faces, as macular oedema distorts the photoreceptors responsible for fine detail.
Sudden floaters or dark spots in the visual field when new retinal vessels bleed, releasing blood into the normally clear vitreous gel.
Flashes of light (photopsia) in the peripheral vision, a warning sign of traction on the retina from fibrovascular membranes.
Dark or empty central patch (scotoma) corresponding to areas of retinal ischaemia or dense macular oedema that have reduced photoreceptor function.
Colour vision disturbance — colours appearing washed out or yellowed — because the cone photoreceptors at the macula are affected by oedema.
Distorted straight lines appearing wavy (metamorphopsia), noticed when looking at grids, door frames, or text, indicating macular swelling.
Reduced contrast sensitivity in dim lighting, making night driving or moving around in low light unexpectedly difficult.
Sudden, painless loss of vision in one eye when a vitreous haemorrhage or traction retinal detachment occurs — this is an emergency.
Gradually shrinking visual field, particularly the peripheral field, in advanced PDR with widespread ischaemia or neovascular glaucoma.
No symptoms at all during the early and moderate non-proliferative stages, which is why screening every 1–2 years is essential in all people with diabetes.
earlyWarningSigns: Slightly blurred vision in one eye that resolves, which may reflect early macular oedema.
Occasional floaters, particularly dark specks, in an otherwise unchanged visual field.
Difficulty adjusting from bright light to dim environments more than before.
Subtle difficulty reading fine print even with glasses that previously worked well.
emergencySigns: Sudden painless loss of vision or a large dark curtain in the visual field — likely vitreous haemorrhage or retinal detachment, both needing urgent ophthalmological assessment within hours.
Rapid onset of new, numerous floaters with a simultaneous decrease in vision in a person with diabetes.
Eye pain with redness and visual loss in a diabetic patient, suggesting neovascular glaucoma from iris neovascularisation.
Any sudden change in vision in a diabetic patient following trauma, even minor, warrants same-day review.

causes.description: Sustained high blood glucose activates protein kinase C, accumulates advanced glycation end-products, and increases reactive oxygen species. Together these impair the retinal pericytes that normally stabilise capillary walls, leading to microaneurysm formation, increased vascular permeability, and progressive ischaemia.

As capillaries close, ischaemic retina releases vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). VEGF stimulates the growth of new but structurally fragile blood vessels that lack the tight junctions of normal retinal vessels, making them prone to haemorrhage and contraction.

Elevated blood pressure increases shear stress on retinal vessel walls, accelerates atherosclerotic changes, and synergises with hyperglycaemia to accelerate pericyte loss and microaneurysm formation. Blood pressure above 130/80 mmHg substantially raises risk of progression.

Elevated serum triglycerides and LDL-cholesterol increase the lipid that leaks from damaged retinal vessels and deposits as hard exudates near the macula. High triglycerides independently predict clinically significant macular oedema.
Concurrent kidney disease correlates strongly with severe retinopathy, possibly because uraemia, anaemia, and hypertension driven by nephropathy independently stress the retinal vasculature.

Duration is one of the strongest independent predictors: after 15–20 years of type 1 diabetes, nearly all patients show some retinopathy. Each additional decade with type 2 diabetes adds about 10–15 percentage points to the prevalence of significant retinopathy.

riskFactors.description: Every 1% absolute reduction in HbA1c reduces the risk of microvascular complications, including retinopathy, by approximately 37% in people with type 2 diabetes (UKPDS). Maintaining HbA1c near 7% (53 mmol/mol) is the primary prevention target.

Systolic blood pressure above 140 mmHg doubles the risk of severe retinopathy. The UKPDS 38 trial found that tight blood pressure control (target < 150/85 mmHg) reduced the risk of diabetic eye disease deterioration by 34%.

The single strongest predictor: retinopathy prevalence rises from about 13% at 5 years to more than 80% after 20 years of type 1 diabetes. Cumulative metabolic exposure appears to be irreversible even with subsequent control.

Type 1 diabetes carries higher lifetime retinopathy rates; however, because type 2 is far more prevalent, it accounts for the majority of vision-threatening cases. Onset of retinopathy in type 2 may predate diagnosis by years.

riskFactors.factor: Pregnancy in a person with pre-existing diabetes
riskFactors.description: Pregnancy can accelerate retinopathy by two to three stages, particularly in the first trimester when rapid glycaemic improvement occurs. Eyes must be examined before conception and each trimester.

Elevated triglycerides and LDL-cholesterol increase risk of macular hard exudates and clinically significant DMO. Fenofibrate treatment in two large trials (FIELD, ACCORD Eye) reduced the need for laser treatment by approximately 30–37%.

Proteinuria and reduced GFR correlate strongly with sight-threatening retinopathy, possibly through shared pathophysiological pathways and the secondary effects of uraemia and anaemia on retinal perfusion.

Low haemoglobin reduces oxygen delivery to the ischaemic retina, worsening ischaemia-driven VEGF release. Correction of anaemia in people with diabetic kidney disease has been associated with slower retinopathy progression in observational studies.
Smoking promotes oxidative stress and vasoconstriction that compound hyperglycaemia-driven endothelial damage; some studies show a 2-fold increase in PDR among smokers with diabetes.

affectedDemographics: Adults with type 1 or type 2 diabetes of any age, with risk rising steeply after 10 years of disease
Working-age adults aged 20–64, who account for the majority of blindness attributable to diabetic retinopathy
South Asian, African, and Hispanic populations, who have higher rates of both diabetes and diabetic microvascular complications
Pregnant women with pre-existing diabetes, in whom retinopathy can rapidly worsen
People with both diabetes and hypertension or kidney disease, who carry compounded microvascular risk
Older adults with long-standing type 2 diabetes, who may have had retinopathy silently progressing for years before diagnosis
diagnosisOverview: The cornerstone of diagnosis is dilated fundus examination, in which both pupils are pharmacologically widened to allow the ophthalmologist or optometrist to inspect the entire retina. Digital fundus photography and optical coherence tomography (OCT) have transformed screening: a 45–60-degree non-mydriatic retinal photograph can detect most clinically significant lesions without dilation, and OCT provides cross-sectional retinal maps that quantify macular oedema with sub-millimetre resolution and greater sensitivity than clinical examination alone. Fluorescein angiography (FA) remains the gold standard for mapping retinal ischaemia, defining the extent of non-perfusion, and guiding laser treatment; it involves an intravenous dye injection and sequential photographs as the dye perfuses the retinal vessels. Swept-source OCT angiography (OCTA), a newer technique, can image the retinal vasculature without dye, identifying microaneurysms and capillary drop-out, and is increasingly used in tertiary centres. The international clinical disease severity scale (mild/moderate/severe NPDR and PDR) is applied based on funduscopic findings and guides treatment decisions. Screening intervals recommended by the AAO Preferred Practice Pattern and the American Diabetes Association Standards of Care are: annual examination from the time of type 2 diabetes diagnosis, and within five years of type 1 onset then annually; more frequent (every six months) if moderate NPDR or DMO is present. OCT of the macula should accompany fundus photography when any macular change is suspected because it detects subretinal and intraretinal fluid before it is clinically visible. Blood pressure, HbA1c, lipid profile, and urinalysis for proteinuria inform systemic risk stratification alongside the ocular findings.

diagnosticTests.test: Dilated fundus examination with slit-lamp biomicroscopy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Allows a detailed stereoscopic view of the optic disc, macula, and peripheral retina to classify retinopathy severity and detect macular oedema, new vessels, or traction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Dilating drops are placed in each eye and take 20–30 minutes to work; your vision will be blurry and bright-light sensitive for 4–6 hours. The clinician then examines your retina with a bright light and lens — no pain involved.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides a permanent photographic record of the retina for grading, monitoring progression over time, and telemedicine screening.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You place your chin on a rest, look at a target, and the camera takes a bright flash photograph. The process takes about 10 minutes and may or may not require dilating drops depending on the system.

diagnosticTests.test: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) of the macula
diagnosticTests.purpose: Produces high-resolution cross-sectional images of the retinal layers, measuring retinal thickness to detect and quantify diabetic macular oedema, subretinal fluid, and vitreoretinal traction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A near-infrared light scan takes a few seconds per eye — completely non-contact and painless. Results are available immediately and show retinal thickness maps in colour.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps retinal blood flow, identifies areas of capillary non-perfusion and leakage, pinpoints the source of macular oedema, and guides laser or injection treatment planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fluorescent dye is injected into a vein in your arm; photographs are taken as the dye circulates through the retinal vessels over about 10–15 minutes. The dye turns urine yellow for about 24 hours; mild nausea can occur.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive retinal vascular imaging detecting microaneurysms, capillary dropout, and neovascularisation without dye injection, increasingly used alongside FA in tertiary assessment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on the same type of camera as standard OCT; no injection needed. The scan takes a few minutes per eye and is entirely painless.

diagnosticTests.test: Visual acuity measurement and contrast sensitivity testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the functional impact of retinopathy on central vision and detects subtle macular changes before the patient notices impairment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard eye chart reading with each eye separately, followed by a contrast test using progressively faded letter charts or a specific screen-based test.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of diabetic retinopathy is stratified by severity and is always underpinned by optimising systemic risk factors — bringing HbA1c as close to target as safely achievable, controlling blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg, and managing dyslipidaemia. For mild to moderate NPDR without DMO, intensive medical management and annual monitoring are the standard of care; no intraocular treatment is required at this stage. When DMO involves the centre of the macula (centre-involving DMO), intravitreal anti-VEGF injections are the first-line treatment, supported by the landmark DRCR.net Protocol T trial, which demonstrated that aflibercept (Eylea), ranibizumab (Lucentis), and bevacizumab (Avastin) all improve visual acuity significantly over observation, with aflibercept showing a modest advantage in eyes with worse baseline acuity. Intravitreal corticosteroid implants (fluocinolone acetonide, dexamethasone) are second-line options, particularly for pseudophakic patients who respond inadequately to anti-VEGF or prefer less frequent visits. For severe NPDR and PDR, pan-retinal photocoagulation (PRP) laser remains a proven, durable treatment for PDR, reducing the risk of severe visual loss by 50% in the Diabetic Retinopathy Study. Anti-VEGF therapy (Protocol S in the DRCR.net) offers a non-inferior outcome to PRP for PDR with a lower side-effect burden, provided injection adherence can be maintained. Vitreoretinal surgery is indicated for non-clearing vitreous haemorrhage, tractional retinal detachment involving or threatening the macula, and severe fibrovascular proliferation unresponsive to injection therapy. The American Academy of Ophthalmology Preferred Practice Pattern (2019 update) and the Royal College of Ophthalmologists (2020 guidelines) set the evidence base for these recommendations.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravitreal anti-VEGF agents (aflibercept, ranibizumab, bevacizumab, faricimab)
medicalTreatments.description: Injected into the vitreous cavity approximately every 4–8 weeks initially, then extended based on response. Anti-VEGF antibodies neutralise VEGF, reducing vascular permeability and regression of new vessels. Faricimab (Vabysmo), dual-targeting VEGF and angiopoietin-2, offers extended dosing intervals up to every 16 weeks in responders.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: DRCR.net Protocol T showed mean gain of +13 ETDRS letters at 1 year with aflibercept in centre-involving DMO; anti-VEGF also reduces PDR severity by 2 or more steps on the DRSS scale in roughly 40% of patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravitreal corticosteroid implants (fluocinolone acetonide 0.19 mg, dexamethasone 0.7 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Slow-release steroid implants reduce vascular inflammation and permeability in DMO. Fluocinolone acetonide (Iluvien) lasts up to 3 years; dexamethasone (Ozurdex) lasts 4–6 months. Main risks are cataract formation and raised intraocular pressure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: FAME trial: fluocinolone 0.19 mg achieved ≥15 ETDRS letter gain in 28.7% of previously treated patients at 24 months versus 16.2% in sham.

medicalTreatments.name: Pan-retinal photocoagulation (PRP) laser
medicalTreatments.description: Approximately 1,200–1,600 laser burns applied to the peripheral retina to destroy ischaemic tissue, reducing VEGF production and causing regression of new vessels. Delivered in one to three sessions under topical anaesthesia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Diabetic Retinopathy Study (DRS): PRP reduces the risk of severe visual loss (< 5/200) by more than 50% in PDR with high-risk characteristics.

medicalTreatments.name: Focal/grid macular laser photocoagulation
medicalTreatments.description: Low-energy laser spots applied directly to leaking microaneurysms (focal) or to a grid pattern over the leaking macula (grid) to reduce DMO. Now largely replaced by anti-VEGF as first-line for centre-involving DMO but still used for non-centre DMO and as adjunctive therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ETDRS trial: macular laser reduced the risk of moderate vision loss from DMO by 50% versus observation; mean visual acuity gain is modest (~1–2 lines) compared with anti-VEGF (~3 lines).

medicalTreatments.name: Fenofibrate (systemic lipid-lowering for adjunctive prevention)
medicalTreatments.description: A fibrate medication given orally to lower triglycerides and modify HDL. Shown in two large randomised controlled trials to independently reduce laser treatment requirements and retinopathy progression regardless of lipid levels.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: FIELD trial: fenofibrate reduced laser treatment requirement by 30% (HR 0.69) in type 2 diabetes over 5 years; ACCORD Eye confirmed a 37% reduction in retinopathy progression rate when combined with simvastatin.

surgicalOptions.name: Pars plana vitrectomy (PPV)
surgicalOptions.description: Microsurgical removal of the vitreous gel, fibrovascular membranes, and blood using fine instruments introduced through small scleral ports. The posterior hyaloid is peeled, traction released, and the retina re-attached if detached; laser endophotocoagulation is typically applied during the same procedure. Performed under local or general anaesthesia as a day-case procedure or brief admission.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Anatomical retinal re-attachment achieved in 80–90% of first-attempt vitrectomy for tractional detachment not involving the fovea; functional outcome (vision preservation) depends strongly on macular status at the time of surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Vitrectomy for non-clearing vitreous haemorrhage
surgicalOptions.description: When haemorrhage from ruptured new vessels prevents adequate anti-VEGF or laser treatment and does not clear spontaneously within 1–3 months, vitrectomy removes the blood, allows direct retinal inspection, and permits endolaser treatment of the bleeding source.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Early Vitrectomy Study found that early vitrectomy (< 6 months) for type 1 PDR with vitreous haemorrhage yielded 25% achieving 20/40 or better vision, versus 15% in the observation arm at 4 years.

alternativeTreatments.name: Intensive glycaemic optimisation
alternativeTreatments.description: The Diabetes Control and Complications Trial (DCCT) and UKPDS established that lowering HbA1c to near-normal prevents the onset of retinopathy and slows progression; this is not an 'alternative' but the primary preventive intervention that modifies the underlying disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sustained-release drug delivery systems (investigational)
alternativeTreatments.description: Port delivery system with ranibizumab (Susvimo) is an implantable refillable drug reservoir approved by the FDA (2021) that delivers continuous anti-VEGF therapy with refills every 24 weeks on average, reducing injection burden in patients with DMO.

alternativeTreatments.name: Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Observational data suggest omega-3 fatty acids may modestly reduce retinopathy progression through anti-inflammatory and anti-angiogenic pathways; randomised trial evidence is insufficient to recommend this as a standalone treatment, but dietary intake of oily fish is reasonable.

whySeeSpecialist: An ophthalmologist with subspecialty training in medical retina or vitreoretinal surgery should manage any patient with moderate or worse NPDR, DMO, or PDR. Primary care physicians and general ophthalmologists can screen and monitor mild NPDR, but the nuanced decisions around anti-VEGF dosing intervals, laser timing, surgical planning, and managing raised intraocular pressure from steroid implants require retina specialist expertise. Patients with rapid progression during pregnancy or with co-existing glaucoma or cataract also need coordinated subspecialty care.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a board-certified ophthalmologist with subspecialty fellowship training in medical retina or vitreoretinal surgery. Look for a practice with in-house OCT, fluorescein angiography, and intravitreal injection capability. For complex cases — traction detachment, florid PDR, combined retinal and glaucoma disease — a vitreoretinal surgeon with high-volume injection and surgical caseload is appropriate. Check whether the practice participates in a diabetic retinopathy screening program and whether they communicate results directly to your endocrinologist or diabetes nurse.
preventionStrategies: Maintain HbA1c as close to 7% (53 mmol/mol) as safely possible — each 1% reduction cuts microvascular complication risk by roughly 37%.
Keep blood pressure below 130/80 mmHg using first-line agents; ACE inhibitors or ARBs are preferred in people with diabetes and any degree of proteinuria.
Attend annual dilated eye examinations from diagnosis for type 2 diabetes, and from 5 years after onset for type 1 — most sight-threatening disease is detected before symptoms appear.
Manage dyslipidaemia; consider fenofibrate in people with elevated triglycerides — the FIELD and ACCORD Eye trials both showed independent ocular benefit.
Stop smoking — tobacco use approximately doubles the risk of severe retinopathy by compounding oxidative stress on the retinal vasculature.
Women with diabetes planning pregnancy should have a pre-conception retinal examination and at least quarterly monitoring during pregnancy.
lifestyleModifications: Exercise regularly — 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity per week improves insulin sensitivity, lowers HbA1c by 0.5–1%, and reduces cardiovascular risk that compounds retinal disease.

Follow a low-glycaemic, plant-forward diet to support glucose and lipid targets; the Mediterranean-style diet reduces HbA1c by approximately 0.5% compared with standard low-fat diets in meta-analyses.
Quit smoking immediately — nicotine replacement or pharmacotherapy is effective; smoking cessation reduces both retinopathy progression risk and cardiovascular mortality.
Limit alcohol, which disrupts glycaemic control and can worsen triglycerides, a direct risk factor for hard exudate formation in the macula.
Self-monitor blood glucose or use continuous glucose monitoring to detect post-meal spikes — reducing glycaemic variability protects the retinal vasculature beyond just reducing mean HbA1c.
Attend all scheduled ophthalmology appointments and report any sudden vision change, new floaters, or flashes between scheduled visits without delay.

recommended: Non-starchy vegetables, legumes, and whole grains to maintain a low glycaemic load and support HbA1c targets
Oily fish two or more times weekly for omega-3 fatty acids with possible anti-angiogenic benefit
Nuts, olive oil, and avocado as healthy fat sources that support lipid targets without raising triglycerides
Adequate dietary antioxidants — lutein and zeaxanthin from leafy greens and eggs — which concentrate in the macula and may support retinal health
avoid: Refined carbohydrates and sugar-sweetened beverages that cause postprandial glucose spikes
Trans fats and processed meats that worsen dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk
Excess dietary sodium, which exacerbates hypertension — a major independent driver of retinopathy
Very high doses of vitamin E supplements — the HOPE trial found 400 IU/day increased heart failure risk without retinal benefit in diabetic patients

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate-intensity aerobic exercise — brisk walking, cycling, swimming — for at least 150 minutes per week lowers HbA1c and blood pressure, both key targets in retinopathy prevention. Resistance training two to three times per week adds further metabolic benefit. Patients with severe NPDR or PDR, active vitreous haemorrhage, or recent laser or vitreoretinal surgery should avoid strenuous activity (heavy Valsalva, contact sports, or high-impact exercise) until cleared by their ophthalmologist, as raised intraocular pressure from exertion could worsen haemorrhage or stress fragile new vessels. For most people in the early stages, there are no exercise restrictions.
dailyManagement: Take blood glucose and blood pressure readings as prescribed and share data with your diabetes care team at each visit.
Use an Amsler grid (a printed square grid) at home weekly to check for new distortion or blind spots at the macula — any change warrants prompt contact with your ophthalmologist.
Keep all ophthalmology and diabetes appointments; retinopathy can progress without symptoms, and the window for effective treatment closes if significant traction or haemorrhage occurs.
Take ophthalmic and systemic medications exactly as prescribed — anti-VEGF injection schedules require adherence because under-treatment allows recurrence of oedema and loss of vision gains.
Inform your diabetes team of any eye changes; adjustments to glycaemic targets may be needed before rapid tightening of control (which can temporarily worsen retinopathy).
Have a low threshold for same-day ophthalmology review if you notice sudden new floaters, flashing lights, or any acute vision change.
prognosis: With systematic screening, optimised systemic risk factor control, and timely treatment, the majority of people with diabetic retinopathy can preserve functional vision throughout their lifetime. The Wisconsin Epidemiologic Study of Diabetic Retinopathy found that among patients with mild to moderate NPDR, progression to PDR over 10 years occurred in roughly 30–40% if poorly controlled but only 10–15% with intensive glycaemic management. Anti-VEGF treatment achieves clinically meaningful visual gain (≥10 ETDRS letters) in approximately 30–40% of centre-involving DMO eyes and stabilises vision in a further 40–50%. Visual prognosis is substantially worse once traction retinal detachment involves the fovea, which underscores the importance of early detection. People with severe NPDR or PDR who engage consistently with treatment have a greater than 90% chance of avoiding legal blindness over a 5-year follow-up period in DRCR.net registry data. The outlook has improved markedly over the past two decades as intravitreal anti-VEGF replaced or supplemented laser as the primary treatment.
recoveryTimeline: After intravitreal anti-VEGF injections, vision improvement in centre-involving DMO is typically detectable by the 4-week follow-up visit and reaches its plateau over 6–12 months with loading doses; many patients require ongoing injections every 8–16 weeks indefinitely. Macular laser carries little recovery time but requires 3–6 months to assess full effect. After vitrectomy for tractional detachment or vitreous haemorrhage, vision may be blurred for weeks owing to gas or silicone oil tamponade; driving and strenuous activity are restricted for 4–8 weeks. Final visual outcome after vitreoretinal surgery is typically assessed at 3–6 months postoperatively.
complications: Vitreous haemorrhage — sudden painless vision loss when fragile new vessels rupture and bleed into the vitreous; mild cases may clear spontaneously over weeks but dense haemorrhage requires vitrectomy.

Tractional retinal detachment — fibrovascular membranes on proliferative vessels contract and peel the retina from the underlying pigment epithelium; if the fovea is detached, vision loss can be permanent without prompt surgery.

Neovascular glaucoma — new vessels growing on the iris (rubeosis iridis) block aqueous drainage, causing a severe acute rise in intraocular pressure with pain, corneal oedema, and rapid visual field loss.

Diabetic macular oedema causing permanent central visual loss — chronic untreated oedema leads to photoreceptor damage and subretinal fibrosis that is not reversible even with successful fluid resolution.

Cataract — accelerated lens opacification from hyperglycaemia, made worse by intravitreal steroids, reduces the quality of retinal imaging and the success of laser and injection treatments, often requiring cataract surgery before retinal procedures.
Legal blindness — defined as best-corrected visual acuity worse than 6/60 (20/200) in the better eye; a preventable outcome in most cases if screening and treatment are accessed in time.

supportResources.description: Plain-English overview of diabetic retinopathy from the U.S. National Eye Institute, covering what it is, symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment options.

supportResources.name: American Diabetes Association — Eye Complications
supportResources.description: ADA patient resources on diabetic eye disease, including how to protect your vision and when to see a specialist.

supportResources.name: NHS — Diabetic Retinopathy
supportResources.description: UK national guidance on diabetic retinopathy symptoms, the NHS Diabetic Eye Screening Programme, and treatment options.

sources.title: Teo ZL et al. Global Prevalence of Diabetic Retinopathy and Projection of Burden through 2045. Ophthalmology 2021

confusedWithConditions.name: Age-related macular degeneration (AMD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AMD occurs in older adults without diabetes; both conditions affect central vision but AMD is driven by drusen and complement dysregulation rather than vascular hyperglycaemia. OCT patterns, risk factors, and treatment differ substantially.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Glaucoma damages the optic nerve through raised intraocular pressure and causes peripheral field loss, not central blurring; neovascular glaucoma can complicate severe PDR, but primary open-angle glaucoma is a distinct disease requiring tonometry and nerve imaging for diagnosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cataracts
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cataracts cloud the lens and reduce overall vision quality with glare and hazing, whereas diabetic retinopathy affects the retinal tissue itself. Both can coexist in diabetic patients — a dense cataract may actually mask the severity of underlying retinopathy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypertensive retinopathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypertensive retinopathy from severely elevated blood pressure produces arteriovenous nicking, copper wiring, and papilloedema rather than the microaneurysms and neovascularisation of diabetic retinopathy; the two can coexist in people with both diabetes and hypertension.

relatedConditions.name: Age-related macular degeneration
relatedConditions.relevance: The other leading cause of central vision loss in adults; overlapping anti-VEGF treatment pathways and both managed by retina specialists.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares ophthalmological management and can complicate PDR through neovascular glaucoma; both require intraocular pressure monitoring in diabetic patients.

relatedConditions.name: Cataracts
relatedConditions.relevance: Accelerated by hyperglycaemia and by steroid treatments for DMO; cataract extraction often precedes or accompanies vitreoretinal surgery for advanced retinopathy.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic nephropathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares microvascular pathophysiology with retinopathy; proteinuria is an independent predictor of severe retinopathy and both conditions track together with duration of diabetes.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 diabetes mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: The underlying cause in the majority of diabetic retinopathy cases; glycaemic control is the primary disease-modifying intervention.

faqs.question: Can diabetic retinopathy be reversed?
faqs.answer: Early-stage retinopathy can partially regress with tight glycaemic and blood pressure control; microaneurysms and mild haemorrhages may resolve. Macular oedema often clears substantially with anti-VEGF treatment. However, once the retina has suffered significant ischaemia, neovascularisation, or traction detachment, the structural damage is not fully reversible — treatment prevents further deterioration rather than restoring lost retinal architecture.

faqs.question: At what stage of diabetes does retinopathy develop?
faqs.answer: Retinopathy can be present at the time of type 2 diabetes diagnosis because the disease is often undetected for years before a formal diagnosis. In type 1 diabetes it rarely appears in the first 5 years but affects most patients after 20 years. Risk accelerates with each year of poorly controlled blood glucose.

faqs.question: How often should a diabetic person have their eyes checked?
faqs.answer: People with type 2 diabetes should have a dilated eye examination at the time of diagnosis and annually thereafter. Those with type 1 diabetes should be first examined within 5 years of onset, then annually. If moderate NPDR or macular oedema is found, examinations every 3–6 months are recommended.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for diabetic retinopathy?
faqs.answer: Optimising HbA1c and blood pressure is the best long-term treatment for preventing progression. For diabetic macular oedema, intravitreal anti-VEGF injections (aflibercept, ranibizumab, bevacizumab, or faricimab) are the current first-line evidence-based treatment. Pan-retinal laser or anti-VEGF injections treat proliferative disease, and vitrectomy is used when haemorrhage or traction detachment occurs.

faqs.question: Does diabetic retinopathy cause blindness?
faqs.answer: Diabetic retinopathy is the leading cause of new blindness in adults of working age globally. However, the majority of blindness from this condition is preventable with regular screening and timely treatment. Most people who engage with care retain functional vision.

faqs.question: Can you drive with diabetic retinopathy?
faqs.answer: Many people with mild or moderate retinopathy retain sufficient vision to drive legally. Vision-threatening PDR, significant macular oedema, or a history of vitreous haemorrhage may fall below legal driving standards; requirements vary by country. After intravitreal injections or laser treatment, driving is typically restricted for 24–48 hours because of the dilating drops used. Always inform the licensing authority if vision is affected.

faqs.question: How are anti-VEGF injections given for diabetic retinopathy?
faqs.answer: Anti-VEGF injections are given in the ophthalmology clinic under topical and, in some centres, subconjunctival anaesthesia. The eye is cleaned with antiseptic, a speculum holds the lids open, and a fine needle delivers the drug into the vitreous cavity through the white of the eye. The procedure takes about 10 minutes and discomfort is usually mild. Most patients require monthly injections initially, extending to every 8–16 weeks as the condition stabilises.

faqs.question: Is diabetic retinopathy painful?
faqs.answer: Diabetic retinopathy itself is painless throughout most of its course. Pain occurs only if neovascular glaucoma develops, where sharply elevated intraocular pressure causes a red, aching eye — this is an emergency. Intravitreal injections cause brief discomfort; laser treatment may cause a mild stinging sensation.

faqs.question: Can exercise worsen diabetic retinopathy?
faqs.answer: Moderate exercise is safe for most people and benefits retinopathy by improving glycaemic and blood pressure control. Patients with severe NPDR, active PDR, vitreous haemorrhage, or very recent laser or vitrectomy should avoid heavy lifting, jarring movements, and contact sports until cleared by their ophthalmologist, as these activities can raise intraocular pressure or stress fragile vessels.

faqs.question: How does blood sugar control affect diabetic retinopathy?
faqs.answer: Blood glucose control is the single most powerful modifiable factor. The DCCT trial showed that intensive insulin therapy in type 1 diabetes reduced the development of retinopathy by 76% and slowed progression by 54%. UKPDS confirmed a 25% reduction in microvascular endpoints per 1% reduction in HbA1c for type 2 diabetes.

faqs.question: What is the difference between non-proliferative and proliferative diabetic retinopathy?
faqs.answer: Non-proliferative retinopathy (NPDR) means abnormal changes are confined to the retinal vessels — microaneurysms, haemorrhages, and exudates — without new vessel growth. Proliferative retinopathy (PDR) means ischaemia has triggered growth of new fragile vessels on the retinal surface or into the vitreous, which can bleed suddenly or pull the retina off.

faqs.question: What is diabetic macular oedema?
faqs.answer: Diabetic macular oedema (DMO) is fluid accumulation at the macula — the small area of the retina responsible for sharp central vision. It can occur at any stage of retinopathy and is the most common cause of vision impairment from diabetes. It is detected on OCT and treated primarily with intravitreal anti-VEGF injections.

faqs.question: Is diabetic retinopathy hereditary?
faqs.answer: Diabetes itself has a strong genetic component, and susceptibility to retinopathy runs in families beyond what is explained by glycaemic control alone. Specific gene variants affecting VEGF signalling and endothelial function have been identified as risk factors. However, the modifiable risk factors — blood glucose, blood pressure, and lipids — have a much larger practical impact than genetics alone.

faqs.question: How is diabetic retinopathy different from age-related macular degeneration?
faqs.answer: Diabetic retinopathy results from hyperglycaemia damaging retinal blood vessels and occurs in younger diabetic patients. AMD results from drusen accumulation and complement-driven atrophy or neovascularisation in older adults without diabetes. Both cause central vision loss but through different mechanisms, with different fundus appearances, treatments, and risk factors.

faqs.question: Can diabetic retinopathy develop if blood sugar is only mildly elevated?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even mildly elevated blood glucose over many years causes cumulative vascular damage. Studies show retinopathy in people with impaired fasting glucose (pre-diabetes) whose HbA1c is in the range 6.0–6.4%. The risk is substantially lower than in frank diabetes but is not zero, particularly in people with concurrent hypertension.

faqs.question: What happens during a diabetic eye screening appointment?
faqs.answer: You receive dilating drops that take 20–30 minutes to act. A technician photographs the retina with a digital camera. The images are then graded, usually remotely by a trained screener or AI-assisted system. Results are sent to you and your GP or diabetologist within 2–4 weeks. Abnormal results are referred to an ophthalmologist.

faqs.question: What are floaters in diabetic retinopathy?
faqs.answer: Floaters are dark specks, strings, or cobwebs that move across the visual field. In diabetic retinopathy they commonly result from small haemorrhages from fragile new vessels bleeding into the vitreous. A sudden shower of new floaters, particularly with reduced vision, indicates a larger haemorrhage and requires urgent same-day ophthalmological review.

faqs.question: Can metformin or other diabetes drugs prevent retinopathy?
faqs.answer: Metformin improves glycaemic control and thereby indirectly reduces retinopathy risk. GLP-1 receptor agonists and SGLT2 inhibitors also lower HbA1c and blood pressure and are associated with reduced diabetic complication rates in large cardiovascular outcomes trials, though direct retinal protection data are limited. No oral diabetes drug has been proven to protect the retina independently of its glucose-lowering effect.

faqs.question: How long does laser treatment for diabetic retinopathy take?
faqs.answer: Pan-retinal laser for PDR is typically delivered in one to three sessions, each lasting 20–30 minutes. Focal macular laser for DMO is completed in a single 10–15 minute session. Vision may be mildly blurred for several hours after dilation, and a reduction in night vision and peripheral field is expected after PRP — this is an intended trade-off to preserve central vision.

faqs.question: Is diabetic retinopathy more common in type 1 or type 2 diabetes?
faqs.answer: Retinopathy affects a higher proportion of people with type 1 diabetes over time — nearly all after 20 years. However, because type 2 diabetes is 10–15 times more prevalent globally, the absolute number of people with vision-threatening retinopathy is far higher in type 2. Both types require equally vigilant screening.

faqs.question: Will I need surgery for diabetic retinopathy?
faqs.answer: Most people with diabetic retinopathy never need surgery. Anti-VEGF injections and laser treat the majority of cases effectively. Vitrectomy surgery is reserved for complications including non-clearing vitreous haemorrhage (usually persistent beyond 1–3 months), tractional retinal detachment threatening or involving the macula, and severe fibrovascular proliferation not responding to injections.

metaTitle: Diabetic Retinopathy: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Diabetic retinopathy damages retinal blood vessels and is the leading cause of blindness in working-age adults. Learn symptoms, diagnosis and treatment options.
